1: A revelation of God: The daughters of Jerusalem now ask where the beloved has gone — they want to help find him. The community that was curious (Song of Solomon 5:9) is now engaged. The church's testimony about Christ — her description of his beauty in Song of Solomon 5:10-16 — has drawn others to seek him. This is the dynamic of witness: when the church speaks authentically about the person of Christ, others are drawn to seek him for themselves. The watching world joins the search.
2: A revelation of God: 'My beloved has gone to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies.' The bride knows where the Beloved is — in the garden, gathering lilies (his own beloved ones, described as lilies in Song of Solomon 2:2). Even in his apparent absence he is at work gathering. Christ's absence from the earth between ascension and parousia is not inactivity: he is interceding (Hebrews 7:25), building (Matthew 16:18), and gathering (John 11:52: to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad).
3: A revelation of God: 'I am my beloved's and he is mine; he grazes among the lilies.' The mutual possession formula of 2:16 is reversed: in Song of Solomon 2:16, the bride says 'my beloved is mine and I am his' — her possession stated first. Here, 'I am my beloved's' comes first — his claim on her precedes her claim on him. The reversal marks spiritual maturation: the soul that grows in Christ-likeness moves from thinking primarily of Christ as 'mine' to understanding herself primarily as 'his.' The possessive order is the theology of sanctification.
4: A revelation of God: 'You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, lovely as Jerusalem, awe-inspiring as an army under banners.' The city-comparisons: Tirzah (the northern capital, later), Jerusalem (the southern capital, city of David). The church is beautiful as the great cities — the centers of civilization and culture. But the final comparison is unexpected: awe-inspiring as an army with banners. The church's beauty is not merely decorative but military — she is a force that inspires awe. Ephesians 6:10-17 (the armor of God) is the NT's development: the church that is beautiful is also equipped for war.
5: A revelation of God: 'Look away — your eyes overwhelm me!' The beloved's eyes are so powerful that the groom asks her to look away — her focused love is overwhelming. The intensity of the mutual gaze: Christ is overwhelmed by the love of his people? This is the language of divine delight, the same astonishment that marks God's covenant love: 'Can a woman forget her nursing child ... Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you' (Isaiah 49:15). The divine-human love relationship is one in which the Creator is genuinely moved by the creature's love.
6: A revelation of God: The second wasf (6:5b-7) repeats elements from the first (Song of Solomon 4:1-3) — hair like a flock of goats, teeth like washed sheep with twins, none missing. The repetition is not artistic laziness but theological insistence: the church's completeness and purity before Christ does not change. The eschatological promise — 'not one missing' — is reaffirmed after the episode of seeking and striving (Song of Solomon 5). The bride is no less beautiful after the night of seeking than before.
7: A revelation of God: The cheeks like pomegranate halves behind the veil — the veil that conceals while suggesting. The church's beauty is partially veiled in this age: 'now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face' (1 Corinthians 13:12). What the veil now hides will be fully revealed in the eschaton: 'when Christ who is your life appears, you also will appear with him in glory' (Colossians 3:4). The pomegranate's rich red behind the veil anticipates the full color of the unveiled face.
8: A revelation of God: Sixty queens, eighty concubines, maidens beyond counting — the vast retinue of the ancient king. The contrast with the unique dove will follow in Song of Solomon 6:9. The numbers anticipate the incomparability of the bride: not that others are without value, but that this one is singular. The NT applies this to the church: 'There is one body and one Spirit ... one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all' (Ephesians 4:4-6). The uniqueness of the relationship between Christ and the church is not diminished by the existence of others.
9: A revelation of God: 'My dove, my flawless one, is unique — her mother's only daughter, treasured by the one who gave birth to her.' The word translated 'flawless' is tam — the same word used of Job (Song of Solomon 1:1: blameless and upright) and of the Passover lamb (Exodus 12:5: without blemish). The church is unique and without blemish before Christ — not because of her inherent perfection but because of what Christ has done: 'he has now reconciled you in his body of flesh by his death, in order to present you holy and blameless and above reproach before him' (Colossians 1:22).
10: A shadow: 'Who is this who appears like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awe-inspiring as an army with banners?' The fourfold description climaxing in 'bright as the sun' — the eschatological glory of the church in her consummated state. Revelation 12:1 applies the same imagery to the woman clothed with the sun: the church in her heavenly glory. 'Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father' (Matthew 13:43, drawing on Daniel 12:3). What the Song describes in the language of natural light the NT promises as the final reality.
11: A revelation of God: 'I went down to the grove of walnut trees to look at the new growth in the valley, to see whether the vines had budded or the pomegranates were in bloom.' The beloved's inspection of the garden for signs of new life — the tender examination of fruitfulness. Christ is the one who comes looking for fruit: the parable of the fig tree in Luke 13:6-9 (the owner who comes looking and finds no fruit, but gives one more season). His attention to growth and fruitfulness in the church is watchful and patient.
12: A revelation of God: 'Before I realized it, my longing had placed me beside my noble kinsman's chariot.' The unexpected transport — longing that moves faster than thought. The spiritual experience of being caught up into nearness with Christ unexpectedly: Paul's 'caught up to the third heaven' (2 Corinthians 12:2: whether in the body or out of the body I do not know — God knows). The soul's longing for Christ sometimes outstrips its own comprehension and finds itself in his presence before it intended to seek him.
13: A shadow: 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite; come back, come back, so we can look at you!' The calling back of the Shulamite — the word Shulamit is the feminine form of Shalom, the woman of peace and wholeness. The church called back from the ends of the earth: 'Come, Lord Jesus' (Revelation 22:20) is the church's cry; but 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite' is the divine call to the bride to return. The final exchange — 'why do you gaze on the Shulamite as at the dance of Mahanaim?' — evokes the two camps of angels at Mahanaim (Genesis 32:1-2): the church is beautiful and wondrous to the heavenly hosts, who long to look into the things of salvation (1 Peter 1:12).
